MASTER MOTION CALENDAR ON APR-08-2021 IN 206, DEFENDANTS REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, PELIN CINAR, M.D., AND KENZO HIROSE, M.D.'S MOTION TO CONTINUE TRIAL DATE. DEVON FAGEL, ESQ. APPEARING FOR PLAINTIFF VIA COURTCALL. JAMES NELSON, ESQ. APPEARING FOR DEFENDANTS VIA COURTCALL. THE COURT GRANTS DEFENDANTS' MOTION TO CONTINUE TRIAL. THE TRIAL DATE OF 5/3/21 IS CONTINUED TO 2/14/22 AT 9:30AM IN DEPT. 206 PER AGREEMENT OF PARTIES. ALL PRETRIAL DEADLINES, DISCOVERY AND MOTION PRACTICE INCLUDING 2034 CCP EXPERT DISCOVERY SHALL BE GOVERNED BY THE NEW TRIAL DATE. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT AND MR. NELSON TO SERVE THE ORDER. PARTIES WAIVED NOTICE. JUDGE: SAMUEL K. FENG; CLERK: R. VELUZ; MATTER NOT REPORTED. (206)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Feb-24-2020 Continued To Apr-22-20 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Mar-04-2020 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Sonja Dahl, Esq. of DONNELLY NELSON, T:925.287.3181, for defendant/moving party;
Devon M. Fagel, Esq. of the LAW OFFICES OF BRUCE G. FAGEL AND ASSOCIATES, T:310.281.8700, for plaintiff/ opposing party.

RULING: The matter was argued. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopted its tentatative ruling on DEFENDANT PELIN CINAR'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT as follows:

Defendant Dr. Cinar's demurrer to the second cause of action for concealment is overruled.

Defendant's argument that the cause of action fails because it is outside the scope of the leave this court gave on September 11, 2019 is rejected. Defendant is correct that, in general, leave to amend by a court after sustaining a demurrer is limited to the cause of action at issue. (See People By and Through Dept. of Public Works v. Clausen (1967) 248 Cal.App.2d 770, 785-86.) However, California has a liberal policy of allowing amendments to pleadings, and the court therefore will address the merits.

Plaintiff adequately alleges a claim for fraudulent concealment. "[T]he elements of an action for fraud and deceit based on concealment are: (1) the defendant must have concealed or suppressed a material fact, (2) the defendant must have been under a duty to disclose the fact to the plaintiff, (3) the defendant must have intentionally concealed or suppressed the fact with the intent to defraud the plaintiff, (4) the plaintiff must have been unaware of the fact and would not have acted as he did if he had known of the concealed or suppressed fact, and (5) as a result of the concealment or suppression of the fact, the plaintiff must have sustained damage." (Marketing West, Inc. v. Sanyo Fisher (USA) Corp. (1992) 6 Cal.App.4th 603, 612-613.) Hahn v. Mirda (2007) 147 Cal.App.4th 740 held that a complaint containing closely similar allegations stated a cause of action for fraud and deceit based on concealment. (Id. at 532.)

Defendant's challenge that the third element is not adequately pled is rejected. Plaintiff alleges that "Cinar, M.D. intended to deceive Plaintiff . . . by concealing the above facts." (Compl. Para. 65.) Contrary to defendant's argument, there is no requirement that a plaintiff plead an improper motive. Defendant's challenge that the fourth element of reliance is not adequately pled is also rejected. Plaintiff alleges that that he "relied on the recommendation for surgery by "[Dr.] Cinar . . . not knowing of the concealment of material facts . . . had the omitted and concealed information been disclosed . . . he would not have agreed to the . . . surgery without further diagnostic procedures." (FAC Para. 66.) Plaintiff's FAC sufficiently alleges that had he been aware of the concealed information, he would have behaved differently. Finally, defendant's reliance on Larson v. UHS of Rancho Springs, Inc. (2014) 230 Cal.App.4th 336 is misplaced, as that case involved determining what statute of limitations applied to plaintiff's claims for battery and intentional infliction of emotional distress, not whether his allegations were sufficient to state a cause of action for fraudulent concealment.

Order signed in open court.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com

Off Calendar Jan-24-2020 Off Calendar
MINI MINUTES:

No appearances.

There being no appearance, the tentative ruling on DEFENDANTS PELIN CINAR, KENZO HIROSE, THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT is adopted as follows:

Defendant Pelin Cinar's demurrer to the first amended complaint's second cause of action is ordered off-calendar for failure to comply with CCP 430.41. That statute requires a "meet and confer in person or by telephone." Attorney Dahl's declaration indicates that has not occurred. The parties are ordered to meet and confer immediately. Defendant has until February 21, 2020 to respond to the complaint.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Dec-05-2019 Continued To Jan-29-20 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Oct-04-2019 Continued To Dec-18-19 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Sep-11-2019 Sustained
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES: Atty. BRONISLAV DRAGANOV, ESQ. of Law Offices of Bruce G. Fagel & Associates (Tel: 310-281-8700) appearing via CourtCall for plaintiff Bruce Lee;
Atty. SONJA M DAHL, ESQ. of Donnelly Nelson Depolo Murray & Efremsky (Tel: 925-287-8181) appearing for defendants Pelin Cinar and Kenzo Hirose

HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS PELIN CINAR AND KENZO HIROSE'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court modifies its tentative ruling to grant leave to amend but otherwise adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendants Dr. Cinar and Dr. Hirose's demurrer to the battery claim is sustained with 20 days leave to amend. Plaintiff does not allege he did not give consent to the surgery, but instead argues that his conditional consent was vitiated when defendant physician did not communicate all the information necessary to consent to surgery. (See Compl. 39-40, 63-66.) The allegations do not support a claim for battery against Dr. Cinar and Dr. Hirose because plaintiff does not allege that his consent was predicated upon any condition. When a doctor fails to meet his due care duty to disclose pertinent information, the action should be pleaded in negligence; however, the battery theory should be reserved for those circumstances when a doctor performs an operation to which the patient has not consented. (Cobbs v. Grant (1972) 8 Cal.3d 229, 240-241; see also Ashcraft v. King (1991) 228 Cal.App.3d 604, 609 ["The plaintiff's claim of battery rested on the theory that although the operation was consented to, the consent was subject to a specific condition: only family-donated blood would be used. If the plaintiff could establish the existence of this condition and its breach by the physician, she would establish a battery."].) Additionally, the Rains case is distinguishable because plaintiff does not allege the surgery was intended to be nontherapeutic. (See Rains v. Superior Court (1984) 150 Cal.App.3d 933, 941.) Defendant Dr. Cinar's demurrer to the IIED claim is sustained without leave to amend. "Whether a defendant's conduct can reasonably be found to be outrageous is a question of law that must initially be determined by the court; if reasonable persons may differ, it is for the jury to determine whether the conduct was, in fact, outrageous. [Citation.] Ordinarily, a medical diagnosis and treatment advice will not be considered outrageous unless they are false and given in bad faith." (Berkley v. Dowds (2007) 152 Cal.App.4th 518 [sustaining demurrer to intentional infliction of emotional distress cause of action where defendant physician urged plaintiff to terminate husband's life support to reduce costs].) In this case, plaintiff fails to plead outrageous conduct as a matter of law. "Liability has been found only where the conduct has been so outrageous in character, and so extreme in degree, as to go beyond all possible bounds of decency, and to be regarded as atrocious, and utterly intolerable in a civilized community." (Cochran v. Cochran (1998) 65 Cal.App.4th 488, 496.) Plaintiff does not allege such intolerable conduct and failing to give plaintiff all the information he believed was necessary before consenting to his surgery cannot support this claim.

Order signed in open court.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Court Reporter: Patty Dowling, CSR# 5388, e-mail: pdowling@sftc.org (302/EPS)
